essential_question: What holds together when you put all ten lessons side by side — and where's the crack?
objective: Revisit and connect the vocabulary and skills from Lessons 1–10 — points through classifying triangles — and surface any gaps before the Unit 01 capstone build and MUS Unit Test I.

content.prompt: List Lessons 1–10 from memory in order, one phrase each. Circle the one you're least sure about — that's where we start.

content.heading: L1–L4 in sixty seconds each
bullets: L1 Points, Lines, Rays & Segments — sorted by how many endpoints each has.
L2 Planes & Sets — coplanar points, union (everything in either set) vs. intersection (only what's shared).
L3 Angles & Angle Measure — vertex, rays, degrees, reading a protractor.
L4 Types of Angles — acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex, sorted by degree range.

content.heading: L5–L7 in sixty seconds each
bullets: L5 Parallel & Perpendicular Lines; Bisectors — never-meet vs. meet-at-90°, and cutting something exactly in half.
L6 Supplementary, Complementary & Vertical Angles — 180°, 90°, and the 'across the X' pair that's always equal.
L7 Transversals & Parallel Lines — corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior angles, and their converses.

content.heading: L8–L10 in sixty seconds each
bullets: L8 Polygons, Quadrilaterals & Perimeter — naming polygons, sorting quadrilaterals, perimeter = sum of sides.
L9 Area of Parallelograms, Triangles & Trapezoids — base × height (and the ÷2 or the average-of-bases moves).
L10 Classifying Triangles — scalene/isosceles/equilateral, acute/right/obtuse, and the triangle inequality.

content.heading: One from each third of the unit

content.preview: Next session: the Unit 01 capstone — a compass/CAD-built polygon with verified perimeter & angle sums.

success_criteria.text: I can name the key vocabulary and tools from Lessons 1–10 (points through classifying triangles)
I can pick the correct formula or angle relationship for a given figure
I can identify which of the ten lessons I need to reteach before the test

questions.prompt: Two angles that share a vertex and a side, and don't overlap, are called…
questions.explain: Adjacent angles sit side by side, sharing a vertex and one ray, with no overlap.

questions.prompt: A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides is a…
questions.explain: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides; a parallelogram has two pairs.

questions.prompt: Two angles are supplementary and one measures 118°. What's the other?
A transversal cuts two parallel lines. Which angle pair is always congruent?
questions.explain: Corresponding angles occupy matching corners at each intersection and are always congruent when the lines are parallel.

questions.prompt: A triangle has sides 4 cm, 5 cm, and 10 cm. Does it close into a triangle? Use the triangle inequality to explain.
questions.answer: No — 4 + 5 = 9, which is less than 10, so the two short sides can't reach the long one.
questions.explain: The triangle inequality requires any two sides to sum to MORE than the third; 9 < 10 fails.

questions.prompt: Exit: which of Lessons 1–10 do you most need to review again before the capstone and the test — name it and one reason why.
